scene_title: A churchyard; in it a tomb belonging to the Capulets

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Give me thy torch, boy: hence, and stand aloof:

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Yet put it out, for I would not be seen.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Under yond yew-trees lay thee all along,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Holding thine ear close to the hollow ground;

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: So shall no foot upon the churchyard tread,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Being loose, unfirm, with digging up of graves,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: But thou shalt hear it: whistle then to me,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: As signal that thou hear’st something approach.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Give me those flowers. Do as I bid thee, go.

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: I am almost afraid to stand alone

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: Here in the churchyard; yet I will adventure.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Sweet flower, with flowers thy bridal bed I strew—

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: O woe! thy canopy is dust and stones;—

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Which with sweet water nightly I will dew,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Or, wanting that, with tears distill’d by moans:

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: The obsequies that I for thee will keep

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Nightly shall be to strew thy grave and weep. The Page whistles.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: The boy gives warning something doth approach.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: What cursed foot wanders this way to-night,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: To cross my obsequies and true love’s rite?

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: What, with a torch! muffle me, night, awhile.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Give me that mattock and the wrenching iron.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hold, take this letter; early in the morning

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: See thou deliver it to my lord and father.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Give me the light: upon thy life, I charge thee,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Whate’er thou hear’st or seest, stand all aloof,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And do not interrupt me in my course.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Why I descend into this bed of death,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is partly to behold my lady’s face;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But chiefly to take thence from her dead finger

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A precious ring, a ring that I must use

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In dear employment: therefore hence, be gone:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But if thou, jealous, dost return to pry

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In what I further shall intend to do,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By heaven, I will tear thee joint by joint

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And strew this hungry churchyard with thy limbs:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The time and my intents are savage-wild,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: More fierce and more inexorable far

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Than empty tigers or the roaring sea.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I will be gone, sir, and not trouble you.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: So shalt thou show me friendship. Take thou that:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Live, and be prosperous: and farewell, good fellow.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: For all this same, I’ll hide me hereabout:

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: His looks I fear, and his intents I doubt.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou detestable maw, thou womb of death,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Gorged with the dearest morsel of the earth,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thus I enforce thy rotten jaws to open,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And, in despite, I’ll cram thee with more food! Opens the tomb.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: This is that banish’d haughty Montague,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: That murder’d my love’s cousin, with which grief,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: It is supposed, the fair creature died;

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And here is come to do some villainous shame

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: To the dead bodies: I will apprehend him. Comes forward.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Stop thy unhallow’d toil, vile Montague!

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Can vengeance be pursued further than death?

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Condemned villain, I do apprehend thee:

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Obey, and go with me; for thou must die.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I must indeed; and therefore came I hither.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Good gentle youth, tempt not a desperate man;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Fly hence, and leave me: think upon these gone;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Let them affright thee. I beseech thee, youth,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Put not another sin upon my head,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By urging me to fury: O, be gone!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By heaven, I love thee better than myself;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For I come hither arm’d against myself:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Stay not, be gone; live, and hereafter say,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A madman’s mercy bade thee run away.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: I do defy thy conjurations,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: And apprehend thee for a felon here.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Wilt thou provoke me? then have at thee, boy! They fight.

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: O Lord, they fight! I will go call the watch.

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: O, I am slain! If thou be merciful,

lines.speaker: PARIS
lines.text: Open the tomb, lay me with Juliet.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In faith, I will. Let me peruse this face.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Mercutio’s kinsman, noble County Paris!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What said my man, when my betossed soul

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Did not attend him as we rode? I think

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: He told me Paris should have married Juliet:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Said he not so? or did I dream it so?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Or am I mad, hearing him talk of Juliet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To think it was so? O, give me thy hand,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: One writ with me in sour misfortune’s book!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I’ll bury thee in a triumphant grave;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A grave? O no! a lantern, slaughter’d youth,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For here lies Juliet, and her beauty makes

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: This vault a feasting presence full of light.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Death, lie thou there, by a dead man interr’d. Laying Paris in the tomb.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: How oft when men are at the point of death

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Have they been merry! which their keepers call

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A lightning before death: O, how may I

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Call this a lightning? O my love! my wife!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Death, that hath suck’d the honey of thy breath,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hath had no power yet upon thy beauty:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou art not conquer’d; beauty’s ensign yet

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And death’s pale flag is not advanced there.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Tybalt, liest thou there in thy bloody sheet?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, what more favour can I do to thee,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Than with that hand that cut thy youth in twain

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To sunder his that was thine enemy?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Forgive me, cousin! Ah, dear Juliet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Why art thou yet so fair? shall I believe

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That unsubstantial death is amorous,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And that the lean abhorred monster keeps

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thee here in dark to be his paramour?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For fear of that, I still will stay with thee;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And never from this palace of dim night

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Depart again: here, here will I remain

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With worms that are thy chamber-maids; O, here

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Will I set up my everlasting rest,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And shake the yoke of inauspicious stars

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: From this world-wearied flesh. Eyes, look your last!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Arms, take your last embrace! and, lips, O you

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The doors of breath, seal with a righteous kiss

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A dateless bargain to engrossing death!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Come, bitter conduct, come, unsavoury guide!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou desperate pilot, now at once run on

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The dashing rocks thy sea-sick weary bark!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Here’s to my love! Drinks. O true apothecary!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thy drugs are quick. Thus with a kiss I die.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Saint Francis be my speed! how oft to-night

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Have my old feet stumbled at graves! Who’s there?

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Here’s one, a friend, and one that knows you well.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Bliss be upon you! Tell me, good my friend,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What torch is yond, that vainly lends his light

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To grubs and eyeless skulls? as I discern,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: It burneth in the Capel’s monument.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: It doth so, holy sir; and there’s my master,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: One that you love.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Who is it?

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Romeo.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: How long hath he been there?

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: Full half an hour.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Go with me to the vault.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I dare not, sir:

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: My master knows not but I am gone hence;

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And fearfully did menace me with death,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: If I did stay to look on his intents.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Stay, then; I’ll go alone. Fear comes upon me:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O, much I fear some ill unlucky thing.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: As I did sleep under this yew-tree here,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I dreamt my master and another fought,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And that my master slew him.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Alack, alack, what blood is this, which stains

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The stony entrance of this sepulchre?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What mean these masterless and gory swords

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To lie discolour’d by this place of peace? Enters the tomb.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo! O, pale! Who else? what, Paris too?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And steep’d in blood? Ah, what an unkind hour

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is guilty of this lamentable chance!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The lady stirs. Juliet wakes.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O comfortable friar! where is my lord?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: I do remember well where I should be,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: And there I am. Where is my Romeo? Noise within.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I hear some noise. Lady, come from that nest

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Of death, contagion, and unnatural sleep:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A greater power than we can contradict

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hath thwarted our intents. Come, come away.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy husband in thy bosom there lies dead;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And Paris too. Come, I’ll dispose of thee

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Among a sisterhood of holy nuns:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Stay not to question, for the watch is coming;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Come, go, good Juliet noise again, I dare no longer stay.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Go, get thee hence, for I will not away.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: What’s here? a cup, closed in my true love’s hand?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Poison, I see, hath been his timeless end:

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: O churl! drunk all, and left no friendly drop

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To help me after? I will kiss thy lips;

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Haply some poison yet doth hang on them,

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: To make die with a restorative.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Thy lips are warm.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Lead, boy: which way?

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: Yea, noise? then I’ll be brief. O happy dagger! Snatching Romeo’s dagger.

lines.speaker: JULIET
lines.text: This is thy sheath ; there rust, and let me die.

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: This is the place; there, where the torch doth burn.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: The ground is bloody; search about the churchyard:

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Go, some of you, whoe’er you find attach.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Pitiful sight! here lies the county slain;

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: And Juliet bleeding, warm, and newly dead,

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Who here hath lain these two days buried.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Go, tell the prince: run to the Capulets:

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Raise up the Montagues: some others search:

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: We see the ground whereon these woes do lie;

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: But the true ground of all these piteous woes

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: We cannot without circumstance descry.

lines.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
lines.text: Here’s Romeo’s man; we found him in the churchyard.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Hold him in safety, till the prince come hither.

lines.speaker: THIRD WATCHMAN
lines.text: Here is a friar, that trembles, sighs, and weeps:

lines.speaker: THIRD WATCHMAN
lines.text: We took this mattock and this spade from him,

lines.speaker: THIRD WATCHMAN
lines.text: As he was coming from this churchyard side.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: A great suspicion: stay the friar too.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: What misadventure is so early up,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: That calls our person from our morning’s rest?

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: What should it be, that they so shriek abroad?

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: The people in the street cry Romeo,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: Some Juliet, and some Paris; and all run,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: With open outcry, toward our monument.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: What fear is this which startles in our ears?

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Sovereign, here lies the County Paris slain;

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: And Romeo dead; and Juliet, dead before,

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Warm and new kill’d.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Search, seek, and know how this foul murder comes.

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: Here is a friar, and slaughter’d Romeo’s man;

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: With instruments upon them, fit to open

lines.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
lines.text: These dead men’s tombs.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O heavens! O wife, look how our daughter bleeds!

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: This dagger hath mista’en—for, lo, his house

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Is empty on the back of Montague—

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: And it mis-sheathed in my daughter’s bosom!

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: O me! this sight of death is as a bell,

lines.speaker: LADY CAPULET
lines.text: That warns my old age to a sepulchre.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Come, Montague; for thou art early up,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: To see thy son and heir more early down.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Alas, my liege, my wife is dead to-night;

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: Grief of my son’s exile hath stopp’d her breath:

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: What further woe conspires against mine age?

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Look, and thou shalt see.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: O thou untaught! what manners is in this,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: To press before thy father to a grave?

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Seal up the mouth of outrage for a while,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Till we can clear these ambiguities,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And know their spring, their head, their true descent;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And then will I be general of your woes,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And lead you even to death: meantime forbear,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And let mischance be slave to patience.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Bring forth the parties of suspicion.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I am the greatest, able to do least,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Yet most suspected, as the time and place

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Doth make against me, of this direful murder;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And here I stand, both to impeach and purge

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Myself condemned and myself excused.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Then say at once what thou dost know in this.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I will be brief, for my short date of breath

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is not so long as is a tedious tale.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo, there dead, was husband to that Juliet;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And she, there dead, that Romeo’s faithful wife:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I married them; and their stol’n marriage-day

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Was Tybalt’s dooms-day, whose untimely death

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Banish’d the new-made bridegroom from this city,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For whom, and not for Tybalt, Juliet pined.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: You, to remove that siege of grief from her,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Betroth’d and would have married her perforce

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To County Paris: then comes she to me,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And, with wild looks, bid me devise some mean

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To rid her from this second marriage,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Or in my cell there would she kill herself.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Then gave I her, so tutor’d by my art,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A sleeping potion; which so took effect

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: As I intended, for it wrought on her

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The form of death: meantime I writ to Romeo,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That he should hither come as this dire night,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To help to take her from her borrow’d grave,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Being the time the potion’s force should cease.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But he which bore my letter, Friar John,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Was stay’d by accident, and yesternight

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Return’d my letter back. Then all alone

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: At the prefixed hour of her waking,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Came I to take her from her kindred’s vault;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Meaning to keep her closely at my cell,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Till I conveniently could send to Romeo:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But when I came, some minute ere the time

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Of her awaking, here untimely lay

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The noble Paris and true Romeo dead.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: She wakes; and I entreated her come forth,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And bear this work of heaven with patience:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But then a noise did scare me from the tomb;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And she, too desperate, would not go with me,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But, as it seems, did violence on herself.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: All this I know; and to the marriage

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Her nurse is privy: and, if aught in this

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Miscarried by my fault, let my old life

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Be sacrificed, some hour before his time,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Unto the rigour of severest law.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: We still have known thee for a holy man.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Where’s Romeo’s man? what can he say in this?

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: I brought my master news of Juliet’s death;

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And then in post he came from Mantua

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: To this same place, to this same monument.

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: This letter he early bid me give his father,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: And threaten’d me with death, going in the vault,

lines.speaker: BALTHASAR
lines.text: If I departed not and left him there.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Give me the letter; I will look on it.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Where is the county’s page, that raised the watch?

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Sirrah, what made your master in this place?

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: He came with flowers to strew his lady’s grave;

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: And bid me stand aloof, and so I did:

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: Anon comes one with light to ope the tomb;

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: And by and by my master drew on him;

lines.speaker: PAGE
lines.text: And then I ran away to call the watch.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: This letter doth make good the friar’s words,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Their course of love, the tidings of her death:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And here he writes that he did buy a poison

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Of a poor ’pothecary, and therewithal

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Came to this vault to die, and lie with Juliet.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Where be these enemies? Capulet! Montague!

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: See, what a scourge is laid upon your hate,

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: That heaven finds means to kill your joys with love.

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: And I for winking at your discords too

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Have lost a brace of kinsmen: all are punish’d.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: O brother Montague, give me thy hand:

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: This is my daughter’s jointure, for no more

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Can I demand.

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: But I can give thee more:

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: For I will raise her statue in pure gold;

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: That while Verona by that name is known,

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: There shall no figure at such rate be set

lines.speaker: MONTAGUE
lines.text: As that of true and faithful Juliet.

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: As rich shall Romeo’s by his lady’s lie;

lines.speaker: CAPULET
lines.text: Poor sacrifices of our enmity!

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: A glooming peace this morning with it brings;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: The sun, for sorrow, will not show his head:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Go hence, to have more talk of these sad things;

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Some shall be pardon’d, and some punished:

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: For never was a story of more woe

lines.speaker: PRINCE
lines.text: Than this of Juliet and her Romeo.

stage_directions.text: Enter Paris, and his Page bearing flowers and a torch.
Enter Romeo and Balthasar, with a torch, mattock, etc.
Enter, at the other end of the churchyard, Friar Laurence, with a lantern, crow, and spade.
Falls on Romeo’s body, and dies.
Enter Watch, with the Page of Paris.
Reenter some of the Watch, with Balthasar.
Reenter others of the Watch, with Friar Laurence.
Enter Capulet, Lady Capulet, and others.

glosses.definition: Paris's light for night-mourning
traditionally planted in graveyards; yew is a symbol of death
the graveyard's loose soil
Paris's elegy — scattering flowers on the grave of his intended bride
cover; tent over the bed
funeral rites
digging tool, like a pickaxe
crowbar
precious task
suspicious
unyielding
savagely wild
Romeo imagines himself worse than natural beasts — despair speaking
belly; mouth
loathsome — of the tomb as monster
the tomb as both grave and womb — paradoxical
proud Montague — Paris's bitter epithet for Romeo
consider the dead inside
Paris dies by Romeo's sword — the fourth death of the play
Paris's dying wish — to lie with the woman he loved
Paris is kin to the dead Mercutio — Romeo recognizes him now, too late
Count Paris — his title

glosses.phrase: writ with me in sour misfortune’s book
glosses.definition: a name written in the ledger of disaster alongside my own
a grave of victory — Romeo's paradoxical epitaph for Juliet's tomb
a small glass-sided structure at the top of a tomb, letting in light
a presence-chamber for ceremonial feasting — Romeo transfigures the tomb
those who watch over a dying man
a sudden brightness said to visit the dying — the play's 'fire and powder'
beauty's banner — the redness of life, still on Juliet's cheek

glosses.phrase: crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks
glosses.definition: Romeo notes (without understanding) the blood still in Juliet — she is alive

glosses.phrase: Tybalt, liest thou there in thy bloody sheet
glosses.definition: Tybalt's corpse shares the tomb — Romeo apologizes to him
Death the lover; personified as Juliet's rival — a classical trope
illicit lover — Death has taken Juliet as his mistress
settle down forever — a phrase from the card-game primero meaning 'stake everything'

glosses.phrase: shake the yoke of inauspicious stars
glosses.definition: Romeo finally throws off fate by suicide — an inversion of the prologue's 'star-cross'd'
an eternal contract with all-consuming death
guide — the poison as pilot
reckless navigator — Romeo's soul
storm-sick weary ship — Romeo's body
the apothecary did sell genuine poison — Romeo's dying tribute

glosses.phrase: Thus with a kiss I die
glosses.definition: Romeo's final line — the kiss that began their sonnet in 1.5 is his farewell
may Saint Francis help me — the Friar's order is Franciscan
unfriendly moment
friar who brings comfort
pollution; deadly air of the tomb
untimely death — both unexpected and beyond time
miser; Juliet's playful-bitter name for Romeo who drank all the poison
to help me die after you
perhaps; by chance
medicine — Juliet calls Romeo's mouth a cure
lucky dagger — Juliet welcomes death as rescue
Juliet's body as the dagger's sheath — the play's final, devastating image
corrode within me as I die — a body-as-steel image
arrest
those suspected
length of life
clandestine wedding
appointed time
an accomplice; in on the secret
dreadful
a gloomy reconciliation — the Prince's closing formulation

glosses.phrase: never was a story of more woe
glosses.definition: the play's final couplet — a heroic couplet that formally closes the tragedy

annotations.title: The pileup — why Paris, why the Friar's late arrival
annotations.body: The tomb scene is a compression — three bodies in forty minutes of stage time. Paris dies first, killed by Romeo who does not recognize him. Romeo dies second, from the apothecary's poison. Juliet dies third, by her husband's dagger, after the Friar arrives moments too late. The critical question is: why does Shakespeare bring Paris back to the tomb? The sources don't require it (in Arthur Brooke's poem, Paris does not visit the grave). Shakespeare's addition lets the scene enact the play's pattern of good intentions producing catastrophic outcomes: Paris comes to mourn; Romeo thinks him an enemy; a corpse is added to the tomb unnecessarily. The Friar's late arrival is a structural device — his explanatory speech to the Watch in lines 229 onward gives the Prince (and audience) the full chronology, confirming that the tragedy is complete and unreversable. The sources require this delivery of truth after the deaths; Shakespeare gives it to the character morally most implicated.
sources: Arthur Brooke, The Tragicall Historye of Romeus and Juliet (1562)
Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)
Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Romeo's last speech — 'Thus with a kiss I die'
annotations.body: Romeo's forty-line speech over Juliet's 'corpse' is the play's most formally wrought dying aria. He catalogues her unchanged beauty ('beauty's ensign yet / Is crimson in thy lips and in thy cheeks'), and the hint of crimson is what the audience knows means life — but Romeo reads it as the persistence of death's beauty. He apologizes to Tybalt, now a body sharing the vault. He invokes 'unsubstantial Death' as Juliet's 'paramour' — the traditional figure of Death the Lover, classical and medieval at once. Then 'Here's to my love! O true apothecary! / Thy drugs are quick. Thus with a kiss I die.' The kiss is the same kiss that began their sonnet in 1.5 — pilgrim's absolution now become suicide. The sonnet-world that opened the play is sealed here.

annotations.title: Juliet's wake and suicide — 'happy dagger'
annotations.body: Juliet wakes to find the Friar bending over her, Romeo's body beside her, the poison vial empty: 'O churl! drunk all, and left no friendly drop / To help me after!' The accusation is playful-tender — the half-joke of a spouse robbed of her share. She then kisses Romeo: 'I will kiss thy lips; / Haply some poison yet doth hang on them.' When the poison fails her, she takes his dagger: 'O happy dagger! / This is thy sheath: there rust, and let me die.' The dagger's sheath is her body — an image that fuses the sexual and the funerary, the wedding bed and the grave. Juliet's death is the fourth and most absolute of the play's four major deaths (Mercutio, Tybalt, Paris, Romeo), and its visual vocabulary is Shakespeare's most compressed image of love and death as inseparable.

annotations.title: The Prince's closing couplets — rhyme as closure
annotations.body: The Prince closes the play in rhyming couplets rather than blank verse. 'A glooming peace this morning with it brings; / The sun, for sorrow, will not show his head: / Go hence, to have more talk of these sad things; / Some shall be pardon'd, and some punished: / For never was a story of more woe / Than this of Juliet and her Romeo.' The choice of couplets is deliberate — couplet closure is the sonnet's own technique, and the play that opened with a sonnet now ends with couplets that formally echo a sonnet's sestet. The famous final line ('For never was a story of more woe...') turns the proper names into the rhyme-word, making the tragedy eponymous by the closing syllable. The form itself performs the final act of commemoration: the lovers' story is inscribed, through rhyme, into collective memory.
sources: Jill L. Levenson, The Oxford Shakespeare: Romeo and Juliet (2000)

trials.prompt: Why is Paris's death in the tomb a notable choice by Shakespeare?
options: It's a historical record of Paris's real death
Shakespeare added Paris's tomb-visit to his source — letting the scene embody the play's pattern of good intentions (Paris's mourning) producing catastrophe (Romeo kills him without recognizing him)
It was required by Elizabethan stage conventions
The actor had no other scenes

trials.prompt: What does Romeo note about Juliet's appearance in the tomb — which the audience understands as dramatic irony?
options: That she is obviously alive
That 'beauty's ensign' is still 'crimson in thy lips' — the audience knows this crimson is life, while Romeo reads it as beauty persisting in death
That she looks unlike herself
Her clothes are dishevelled

trials.prompt: Juliet calls Romeo 'O churl! drunk all, and left no friendly drop'. The tone is —
options: Straight fury
Half-playful, half-desolate — the tenderness of a spouse cheated of her share, inflected by despair
Mockery
Indifference

trials.prompt: Juliet's 'O happy dagger! / This is thy sheath: there rust, and let me die' compresses —
options: A wartime allusion
A sexual-funerary image — body as sheath, dagger's entry fusing wedding-bed and grave into one image
A religious oath
A joke about ironmongery

trials.prompt: Why does the Prince end the play in rhymed couplets rather than blank verse?
options: Shakespeare ran out of blank verse material
Couplets formally echo the sonnet form that opened the play, commemorating the story by enclosing it in rhyme — 'more woe / Romeo' is the play's epitaph by rhyme-syllable
Elizabethan law required couplets at the end of plays
It is an editorial addition

trials.prompt: The Prince says 'Some shall be pardon'd, and some punished.' This acknowledges —
options: That the feud is now over without consequence
That justice remains imperfect — not all wrongs can be remedied, and the living must still be sorted out; but the cost is the children
That he intends to execute the Friar
That the Nurse will be jailed
